item.title: Cisco CVE-2026-20230 Exploited, FortiBleed Harvests Credentials, macOS ClickFix Pushes AMOS
item.content:encoded:
FortiBleed Targeted FortiGate Firewalls in 110 Million-Credential Harvesting Operation — Must Read

A Russian-speaking initial access broker is linked to FortiBleed, a large credential-harvesting campaign that compromised FortiGate firewalls and other internet-facing systems to collect more than 110 million credentials. The operation used mass scanning, brute forcing, and a custom FortigateSniffer tool to steal cleartext credentials and hashes for resale, lateral movement, and further intrusions.

  • A Russian-speaking initial access broker is assessed to be behind FortiBleed, a large-scale credential-harvesting operation that has targeted over 430,000 FortiGate firewalls globally since February 2026.
  • The campaign collects credential lists, searches for exposed services, brute-forces accessible systems, and deploys bespoke sniffers on compromised firewalls to capture cleartext and hashed credentials.
  • A Golang-based tool called FortigateSniffer uses the FortiOS built-in diagnostic command “diagnose sniffer packet” to passively capture authentication traffic across 24 protocols from compromised appliances.
  • SOCRadar said FortiBleed appears to be part of a broader multi-vendor initial access operation targeting Fortinet devices, Synology NAS, Sophos firewalls, RDWeb portals, Citrix SSL-VPNs, and MS-SQL servers.
  • The attackers are estimated to have launched at least 659 credential-harvesting pipelines on May 31 and June 15, 2026, identifying more than 110 million credentials including RADIUS credentials, NTLM hashes, Kerberos hashes, and MySQL authentication tokens.

Cisco Unified CM flaw CVE-2026-20230 now exploited in attacks — Must Read

Cisco Unified Communications Manager flaw CVE-2026-20230 is now being exploited, with attackers able to abuse an SSRF bug to write files that can lead to root-level compromise. Cisco patched the issue on June 3, and defenders should update urgently as public exploit details are now available.

  • Cisco Unified Communications Manager and Unified Communications Manager Session Management Edition are affected by CVE-2026-20230, a high-severity server-side request forgery flaw.
  • Cisco released security updates for CVE-2026-20230 on June 3 and warned that successful exploitation could allow attackers to write files that could later be used to gain root privileges.
  • Defused reported that CVE-2026-20230 is now being actively exploited from a single IP address using file:// payloads to create files on affected devices.
  • The observed proof-of-concept activity attempted to write a text file named /tmp/cve-2026-20230-test.txt, suggesting the current exploitation is reconnaissance in nature.
  • SSD Secure published a technical write-up and proof-of-concept exploit showing how Webdialer URL handling can be abused to write arbitrary files and achieve remote code execution.

Healthtech firm Xolis suffers data breach impacting 1.4 million people — Must Read

Xsolis says a targeted phishing attack gave attackers access to files containing sensitive personal and health information for nearly 1.4 million people. The company has contained the incident, reset passwords, added security measures, and is offering affected individuals 12 months of identity monitoring through Kroll.

  • Xsolis says a targeted phishing attack on January 20, 2026 led to unauthorized activity in a limited portion of its environment that it detected on January 22, 2026.
  • The attackers accessed certain files containing customer information, including names, addresses, dates of birth, health insurance information, Social Security numbers, and medical treatment information.
  • Data provided to the U.S. Department of Health and Human Services indicates that 1,396,519 people are impacted by the incident.
  • Xsolis says it contained the activity, launched an investigation with external cybersecurity experts, reported the incident to law enforcement, and is notifying potentially affected individuals by mail.
  • The company says it reset passwords for all users and key accounts, increased system monitoring, strengthened credential management, accelerated employee security training, and is offering 12 months of identity monitoring and identity theft restoration services through Kroll.

New macOS ClickFix attack silently mounts DMGs to push infostealer — Should Read

A new macOS ClickFix campaign tricks users with fake CAPTCHA pages into pasting Terminal commands that silently download, mount, and launch DMG-based Atomic macOS Stealer malware. The malware steals passwords, browser data, crypto wallets, Keychain files, messaging app data, and documents, so users should never run website-provided Terminal commands they do not fully understand.

  • Palo Alto Networks Unit 42 discovered a macOS ClickFix campaign that uses fake CAPTCHA pages to instruct users to open Terminal and paste a malicious command.
  • The command downloads a DMG file from an attacker-controlled server, silently mounts it with macOS hdiutil, searches for an .app or .pkg, and launches it with the macOS open command.
  • The campaign delivers Atomic macOS Stealer, which steals browser credentials, browser history, authentication tokens, cryptocurrency wallet data, Apple Keychain database files, messaging app data, Apple Notes databases, and user documents.
  • The malware displays a fake System Preferences authentication prompt to trick the user into entering their password.
  • Palo Alto observed command-and-control servers at svs-verificationdate[.]beer and 196.251.107[.]171.

VA OIG Identifies Lack of Oversight of VA GenAI Chat Tools — Should Read

A VA OIG review found that Department of Veterans Affairs staff use of generative AI chat tools lacks sufficient safeguards and oversight, creating potential patient safety and sensitive data exposure risks. Health-ISAC points healthcare organizations to AI governance guidance to help manage these risks.

  • A review of Department of Veterans Affairs staff use of generative artificial intelligence tools identified potential patient safety risks from a lack of safeguards and oversight.
  • Health-ISAC said concern is growing that sensitive patient data may be shared with publicly accessible chatbots as generative AI tools are used more widely in healthcare.
  • Health-ISAC said AI tools could generate output that puts patients at risk of harm or even death.
  • Health-ISAC and the Health Sector Coordinating Council Cybersecurity Working Group issued guidance earlier this year on developing effective AI governance frameworks.
  • The article directs readers to Health-ISAC’s white paper, “Policies and Safeguards for a Safe Use of AI,” and the HSCC Health Industry AI Cyber Governance Framework Implementation Guide.

The post-quantum EO is an important milestone. Now it’s time to get to work — Should Read

Cloudflare welcomes the new U.S. executive order setting 2030–2031 deadlines for federal post-quantum cryptography migration, but argues agencies and vendors must start now, especially on public Internet traffic and authentication. The key takeaway is that post-quantum readiness cannot wait for full inventories or future guidance because harvest-now-decrypt-later risks already exist.

  • President Trump signed Executive Order 14409 on June 22, 2026, setting deadlines of December 31, 2030, for federal agencies to transition their most sensitive systems to post-quantum encryption and December 31, 2031, for post-quantum authentication.
  • The order applies binding deadlines to federal High Value Assets and high impact systems, while National Security Systems are excluded because they are on a separate NSA-managed track with deadlines between 2030 and 2033.
  • The article says post-quantum encryption is needed now to prevent attackers from collecting encrypted traffic today and decrypting it later once powerful quantum computers are available.
  • The article argues that post-quantum authentication is a larger migration challenge because it requires coordinated upgrades across clients, servers, certificate authorities, certificate transparency logs, root stores, and browsers.
  • Cloudflare recommends that organizations protect public Internet traffic now, update procurement requirements to demand post-quantum support, use a quantum impact inventory to prioritize migration, and begin planning for post-quantum authentication.

Five Eyes agencies sound alarm about AI’s threat to cybersecurity — May Read

Five Eyes cyber agencies warned that frontier AI could transform offensive and defensive cyber capabilities within months, shrinking the time between vulnerability discovery and exploitation. They urged business leaders to urgently reduce attack surfaces, harden access controls, speed patching, retire legacy systems, and strengthen cyber resilience.

  • Five Eyes intelligence agencies warned that rapidly evolving AI is an urgent cybersecurity threat that business leaders must address immediately.
  • The agencies said frontier AI models could exceed current industry expectations within months and transform both offensive and defensive cyber capabilities.
  • The announcement said AI lowers barriers for malicious actors, increases the speed and complexity of attacks, and shrinks the window between vulnerability discovery and exploitation.
  • The agencies urged leaders to reduce attack surfaces, limit unnecessary access and external connectivity, speed up patching, address unsupported legacy systems, and strengthen access controls.
  • The announcement recommended that business leaders fund and empower internal cyber leaders, follow evolving guidance, adopt secure-by-design and secure-by-default approaches, and maintain defense in depth.

Windows 11 KB5095093 update rolls out new Point-in-Time restore feature — May Read

Microsoft’s optional Windows 11 KB5095093 preview update for versions 24H2 and 25H2 begins rolling out a new Point-in-Time Restore feature that can roll back the full PC state using recent restore points. The update also includes many fixes and improvements, including a Recycle Bin filename bug fix, though a known issue with third-party apps launching Office documents remains.

  • Microsoft released the optional KB5095093 preview cumulative update for Windows 11 24H2 and 25H2 as part of its monthly non-security preview update schedule.
  • The update begins rolling out Point-in-Time Restore, which lets users restore a Windows PC, including apps, settings, and personal files, to a recent automatic restore point.
  • For consumers, Point-in-Time Restore creates new restore points every 24 hours and deletes them after 72 hours or when allocated storage space runs out.
  • KB5095093 fixes a known Recycle Bin issue where the confirmation dialog could show an internal file name instead of the original file name when permanently deleting a file.
  • Microsoft says a known issue remains where third-party applications may be unable to launch Microsoft Office applications or open documents, and affected users should open the application or document directly.

Silent Push partners with CDL Cyber Intelligence to bring preemptive defense to the United Kingdom — Optional

Silent Push has partnered with UK-based CDL Cyber Intelligence to offer preemptive threat intelligence that identifies attacker infrastructure before campaigns reach targets. The partnership focuses on giving UK organizations earlier warning against state-linked and other threats using Silent Push’s Context Graph and Traffic Origin capabilities.

  • Silent Push has entered a strategic partnership with CDL Cyber Intelligence, an intelligence-led cybersecurity firm serving organisations across the UK and Europe.
  • CDL Cyber Intelligence’s team includes former intelligence officers, ethical hackers, and security engineers with experience in threat intelligence, incident response, attack surface management, and dark web monitoring.
  • Silent Push says its Context Graph tracks infrastructure creation and connections across DNS, WHOIS, certificates, and hosting data to surface Indicators of Future Attack before campaigns reach targets.
  • The article states that CDL’s clients get an average of 157 days of lead time before a campaign reaches their perimeter.
  • Silent Push says its Traffic Origin capability identifies the true upstream country of origin controlling a connection, even when the observed IP appears clean.

item.title: Federal PQC deadlines, Scattered Spider plea, and AI agent supply-chain risks
LastPass Klue breach, Scattered Spider pleas, and Miasma supply chain fallout
Malicious npm PostCSS Packages and BioShocking AI Browser Guardrail Bypass
WhatsApp phishing campaign abuses fake business docs to install remote access tools
PixelSmash RCE, FortiBleed Credential Harvesting, and ShapedPlugin Backdoor Highlight Supply Chain and AI Agent Threats
PixelSmash FFmpeg RCE (CVE-2026-8461), FortiBleed Credential Sniffer, ShapedPlugin Backdoor, and AI Agent Compromise Vectors
